title: AI Text Rendering & Streaming UI
description: Rendering large AI/LLM responses in Compose: streaming text, markdown, selection, and performance optimization
lesson:
The Problem — Large Text Rendering Performance

AI/LLM responses can be thousands of tokens, arriving incrementally via streaming (SSE or WebSocket). The naive approach is simple but devastating for performance:

// NAIVE APPROACH — causes jank! @Composable fun ChatResponse(viewModel: ChatViewModel) { val response by viewModel.streamingResponse.collectAsState() Text(text = response) // Recomposes on EVERY chunk }

Every time a new chunk arrives (every ~50ms), the entire Text composable recomposes. For a response that has grown to 5,000 characters, Compose must re-measure and re-layout all of it — text measurement is O(n) on the character count. This causes visible jank as the response grows.

Streaming Token Arrives (~every 50ms) │ ▼ StateFlow.update { old + newChunk } │ ▼ Recomposition triggered │ ▼ Text(response) — entire string re-measured │ ▼ Layout pass: word-wrap 5,000+ chars ~30ms │ ▼ Frame budget: 16ms — EXCEEDED → dropped frame (jank) At 2,000 chars: ~10ms measure → OK At 5,000 chars: ~30ms measure → jank starts At 10,000 chars: ~60ms measure → severe jank
The core problem: Text measurement cost grows linearly with string length, but streaming adds new text every 50ms. After a few paragraphs, measurement alone exceeds the 16ms frame budget.

BasicText vs Text vs AnnotatedString

Compose offers two text composables with different overhead levels:

Text (Material3)BasicText (Foundation)
Packageandroidx.compose.material3androidx.compose.foundation.text
What it doesWraps BasicText + applies themeRaw text rendering, no theme
OverheadLocalTextStyle merge, provideTextStyle, selection handling, Material rippleMinimal — just measures and draws
Use forMost UI text (buttons, labels, cards)Performance-critical large content
// Material3 Text — convenient, applies theme styling @Composable fun ThemedResponse(text: String) { Text( text = text, style = MaterialTheme.typography.bodyLarge, color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onSurface ) } // BasicText — lower overhead for large content @Composable fun RawResponse(text: String) { BasicText( text = text, style = TextStyle( fontSize = 16.sp, color = Color(0xFF1C1B1F), lineHeight = 24.sp ) ) }

Under the hood, Text calls BasicText after merging LocalTextStyle from the theme. For a single text element the difference is negligible, but when rendering a streaming response that recomposes 20 times per second, removing that overhead helps.

AnnotatedString is the third player — it’s not a composable but a data type that both Text and BasicText accept. It lets you apply mixed styles (bold, italic, colored spans, clickable links) within a single text block, similar to Android’s SpannableString. Essential for rendering markdown-formatted AI responses.

Streaming Text — Incremental Rendering Patterns

There are four main patterns for rendering streaming AI text. Each trades complexity for performance:

Pattern 1: Append to Single String

Simple, but full recomposition + re-measurement on every chunk:

// Simple but slow for long responses @Composable fun SimpleStreamingText(flow: Flow<String>) { var fullText by remember { mutableStateOf("") } LaunchedEffect(Unit) { flow.collect { chunk -> fullText += chunk // triggers recomposition } } Text(text = fullText) // re-measures ALL text every chunk }

Pattern 2: List of Chunks in LazyColumn

Each chunk is a separate composable — only new items compose, existing ones are skipped:

// Best for very long responses (thousands of lines) @Composable fun ChunkedStreamingText(flow: Flow<String>) { val chunks = remember { mutableStateListOf<String>() } val listState = rememberLazyListState() LaunchedEffect(Unit) { flow.collect { chunk -> chunks.add(chunk) } } // Auto-scroll to bottom as new chunks arrive LaunchedEffect(chunks.size) { if (chunks.isNotEmpty()) { listState.animateScrollToItem(chunks.lastIndex) } } LazyColumn(state = listState) { items( count = chunks.size, key = { it } // stable keys = no recomposition of old items ) { index -> BasicText( text = chunks[index], style = TextStyle(fontSize = 16.sp, lineHeight = 24.sp) ) } } }

Pattern 3: AnnotatedString Builder for Rich Formatting

Supports markdown-style formatting (bold, code, etc.) in a single text block:

// Rich text with inline formatting @Composable fun RichStreamingText(annotatedText: AnnotatedString) { SelectionContainer { BasicText( text = annotatedText, style = TextStyle(fontSize = 16.sp, lineHeight = 24.sp) ) } }

Pattern 4: Paragraph-Chunked Text

Split large text into paragraphs, each in its own composable. Only the last paragraph recomposes as new text arrives:

// Split into paragraphs — only last one recomposes @Composable fun ParagraphChunkedText(fullText: String) { val paragraphs = remember(fullText) { fullText.split("\n\n").filter { it.isNotBlank() } } Column { paragraphs.forEachIndexed { index, paragraph -> key(index) { // stable key = completed paragraphs don't recompose BasicText( text = paragraph, style = TextStyle(fontSize = 16.sp, lineHeight = 24.sp), modifier = Modifier.padding(bottom = 12.dp) ) } } } }
PatternComplexityPerformanceBest For
1. Single StringTrivialPoor for long textShort responses (<500 chars)
2. LazyColumn ChunksLowExcellentVery long responses, chat UIs
3. AnnotatedStringMediumMediumRich formatted text
4. Paragraph-ChunkedMediumGoodMedium-length formatted responses
The LazyColumn approach (Pattern 2) is best for very long responses because it only composes visible items. Off-screen chunks are disposed entirely. Combined with stable keys, existing chunks never recompose.

Markdown Rendering in Compose

AI responses often contain markdown: **bold**, *italic*, `code`, headers, and fenced code blocks. You need to convert this to AnnotatedString for styled rendering.

Simple Markdown → AnnotatedString Parser

// Lightweight markdown parser for inline formatting fun parseMarkdown(markdown: String): AnnotatedString { return buildAnnotatedString { var i = 0 while (i < markdown.length) { when { // Bold: **text** markdown.startsWith("**", i) -> { val end = markdown.indexOf("**", i + 2) if (end != -1) { withStyle(SpanStyle(fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold)) { append(markdown.substring(i + 2, end)) } i = end + 2 } else { append(markdown[i]) i++ } } // Inline code: `code` markdown[i] == '`' -> { val end = markdown.indexOf('`', i + 1) if (end != -1) { withStyle(SpanStyle( fontFamily = FontFamily.Monospace, background = Color(0xFFE8E8E8), fontSize = 14.sp )) { append(markdown.substring(i + 1, end)) } i = end + 1 } else { append(markdown[i]) i++ } } // Italic: *text* markdown[i] == '*' -> { val end = markdown.indexOf('*', i + 1) if (end != -1) { withStyle(SpanStyle(fontStyle = FontStyle.Italic)) { append(markdown.substring(i + 1, end)) } i = end + 1 } else { append(markdown[i]) i++ } } // Header: # at start of line markdown[i] == '#' && (i == 0 || markdown[i - 1] == '\n') -> { var level = 0 while (i + level < markdown.length && markdown[i + level] == '#') level++ val lineEnd = markdown.indexOf('\n', i).let { if (it == -1) markdown.length else it } val headerText = markdown.substring(i + level, lineEnd).trim() val fontSize = when (level) { 1 -> 24.sp; 2 -> 20.sp; 3 -> 18.sp else -> 16.sp } withStyle(SpanStyle(fontSize = fontSize, fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold)) { append(headerText) } append("\n") i = if (lineEnd < markdown.length) lineEnd + 1 else lineEnd } else -> { append(markdown[i]) i++ } } } } }

Code Blocks with Syntax Highlighting

// Fenced code block rendering @Composable fun CodeBlock(code: String, language: String = "") { val annotatedCode = remember(code) { buildAnnotatedString { withStyle(SpanStyle( fontFamily = FontFamily.Monospace, fontSize = 13.sp, color = Color(0xFFD4D4D4) )) { append(code) } // Add keyword highlighting for known languages if (language == "kotlin" || language == "kt") { val keywords = listOf("fun ", "val ", "var ", "class ", "if ", "else ", "when ", "return ", "suspend ", "override ", "private ", "public ") keywords.forEach { kw -> var idx = code.indexOf(kw) while (idx != -1) { addStyle( SpanStyle(color = Color(0xFF569CD6)), idx, idx + kw.length - 1 ) idx = code.indexOf(kw, idx + 1) } } } } } Surface( color = Color(0xFF1E1E1E), shape = RoundedCornerShape(8.dp), modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth().padding(vertical = 8.dp) ) { BasicText( text = annotatedCode, modifier = Modifier.padding(12.dp) ) } }

Libraries for Production Markdown

compose-markdown (mikepenz/multiplatform-markdown-renderer): Pure Compose markdown renderer. Supports full CommonMark spec, code blocks, tables, images. Best for Compose-first apps.

Markwon: View-based markdown library. Mature, full-featured. Use with AndroidView interop in Compose:
AndroidView(factory = { ctx -> TextView(ctx).also { Markwon.create(ctx).setMarkdown(it, md) } })

For most AI chat apps, compose-markdown is the better choice since it avoids the interop overhead.

SelectionContainer — Copy/Paste

By default, text in Compose is not selectable. Wrap with SelectionContainer to enable select-and-copy:

// Enable text selection for all children @Composable fun SelectableResponse(text: String) { SelectionContainer { Text( text = text, style = MaterialTheme.typography.bodyLarge ) } } // Multiple text blocks — selection spans across them @Composable fun SelectableChat(messages: List<String>) { SelectionContainer { Column { messages.forEach { msg -> Text(text = msg, modifier = Modifier.padding(8.dp)) } } } }
SelectionContainer + LazyColumn limitation: Each LazyColumn item is independently composed and disposed. Selection does NOT span across LazyColumn items — you can only select within a single visible item. This is a fundamental limitation because LazyColumn disposes off-screen items, so their text state doesn’t exist to be selected.

"Copy Response" Button Pattern

Users expect a one-tap copy button (like ChatGPT). Store the plain text alongside any formatted version:

// Store both plain and formatted versions data class AiMessage( val plainText: String, // for clipboard val formatted: AnnotatedString, // for display val isStreaming: Boolean = false ) @Composable fun MessageBubble(message: AiMessage) { val clipboardManager = LocalClipboardManager.current Column { SelectionContainer { BasicText(text = message.formatted) } if (!message.isStreaming) { IconButton(onClick = { clipboardManager.setText(AnnotatedString(message.plainText)) }) { Icon(Icons.Default.ContentCopy, contentDescription = "Copy") } } } }
Gotcha: BasicText works inside SelectionContainer just like Text does. Both support selection when wrapped. The key requirement is that the SelectionContainer must be an ancestor in the composition tree.

Performance Optimization for Streaming

The key insight for streaming performance: isolate the changing state so the parent composable does not recompose when the streaming text changes.

Isolate Streaming Text in Its Own Composable

// BAD — parent reads the state, parent + child both recompose @Composable fun ChatScreen(viewModel: ChatViewModel) { val response by viewModel.response.collectAsState() // parent reads state Column { Header() // recomposes unnecessarily! InputField() // recomposes unnecessarily! Text(response) // this is the only thing that needs to update } } // GOOD — state is read inside child, parent never recomposes @Composable fun ChatScreen(viewModel: ChatViewModel) { Column { Header() // never recomposes during streaming InputField() // never recomposes during streaming StreamingResponseText(viewModel.response) // only this recomposes } } @Composable fun StreamingResponseText(responseFlow: StateFlow<String>) { val text by responseFlow.collectAsState() // state read HERE, not in parent BasicText( text = text, style = TextStyle(fontSize = 16.sp) ) }

derivedStateOf — Throttle Recompositions

// Only recompose when text grows by a meaningful amount @Composable fun ThrottledStreamingText(responseFlow: StateFlow<String>) { val fullText by responseFlow.collectAsState() // derivedStateOf: only triggers recomposition when the derived value changes val displayText by remember { derivedStateOf { fullText // still shows full text, but... } } // Alternatively: only recompose every ~100 characters val textSnapshot by remember { derivedStateOf { fullText.length / 100 // integer division = changes less often } } // Use textSnapshot as a key to control recomposition frequency val displayedText = remember(textSnapshot) { fullText } BasicText( text = displayedText, style = TextStyle(fontSize = 16.sp) ) }

movableContentOf for Complex Content

// movableContentOf preserves state when content moves in the tree @Composable fun AdaptiveChat(isFullScreen: Boolean, content: @Composable () -> Unit) { val movableContent = remember { movableContentOf(content) } if (isFullScreen) { Box(Modifier.fillMaxSize()) { movableContent() } } else { Card(Modifier.padding(16.dp)) { movableContent() } } // The streaming text inside content keeps its state // even when switching between full-screen and card layout }
Key rule: Never read streaming state in a parent composable. Pass the StateFlow (not the value) down to a leaf composable that reads it. This confines recomposition to the smallest possible scope.

Cursor / Typing Animation

A blinking cursor at the end of streaming text (like ChatGPT) provides visual feedback that the response is still generating:

// Blinking cursor composable for streaming text @Composable fun StreamingTextWithCursor( text: String, isStreaming: Boolean, modifier: Modifier = Modifier ) { // Infinite blink animation val infiniteTransition = rememberInfiniteTransition(label = "cursor") val cursorAlpha by infiniteTransition.animateFloat( initialValue = 1f, targetValue = 0f, animationSpec = infiniteRepeatable( animation = tween(durationMillis = 500, easing = LinearEasing), repeatMode = RepeatMode.Reverse ), label = "cursorBlink" ) // Build text + cursor val displayText = remember(text, isStreaming, cursorAlpha) { buildAnnotatedString { append(text) if (isStreaming) { withStyle(SpanStyle( color = Color.Black.copy(alpha = cursorAlpha), fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold )) { append("█") // block cursor character } } } } BasicText( text = displayText, style = TextStyle(fontSize = 16.sp, lineHeight = 24.sp), modifier = modifier ) }
// Usage in a chat screen @Composable fun AiResponseBubble(viewModel: ChatViewModel) { val response by viewModel.response.collectAsState() val isStreaming by viewModel.isStreaming.collectAsState() Card( modifier = Modifier .fillMaxWidth() .padding(horizontal = 16.dp, vertical = 4.dp), colors = CardDefaults.cardColors( containerColor = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surfaceVariant ) ) { StreamingTextWithCursor( text = response, isStreaming = isStreaming, modifier = Modifier.padding(12.dp) ) } }
InfiniteTransition is lifecycle-aware — the animation automatically pauses when the composable leaves the screen and resumes when it returns. No manual cleanup needed. The cursor blink runs at ~2 Hz (500ms per phase), matching the standard cursor blink rate users expect.

questions.title: BasicText vs Text Performance
questions.description: You're rendering a 10,000-character streaming AI response that updates every 50ms. Which composable should you prefer and why?
options: Text — it handles theming automatically so you write less code
BasicText — it skips the Material theme overhead (LocalTextStyle merge, provideTextStyle, selection handling) that Text adds on every recomposition
Neither — use AndroidView with a TextView for large text
BasicText — because Text does not support AnnotatedString
questions.explanation: BasicText is the lower-level composable that Text wraps. Text adds Material theme merging, LocalTextStyle resolution, and other conveniences on every composition. For a composable that recomposes 20 times per second with large text, removing that overhead matters. Note that both support AnnotatedString — option D is incorrect.
hints: Text is a wrapper around BasicText with Material styling
Consider what work happens on EVERY recomposition, not just the first

questions.title: Streaming Pattern Selection
questions.description: You're building a chat UI where AI responses can be up to 50,000 characters (many paragraphs). Which streaming pattern is most appropriate?
options: Pattern 1: Append to a single mutableStateOf string and render with one Text composable
Pattern 3: Build a single AnnotatedString with all the formatting
Pattern 2: Collect chunks into a mutableStateListOf and render in a LazyColumn, one item per chunk
Pattern 4: Split into paragraphs in a Column with key() for each
questions.explanation: For very long responses (50,000 chars), the LazyColumn approach is best. LazyColumn only composes visible items — off-screen chunks are disposed entirely, so memory usage stays bounded. With stable keys, previously-rendered chunks never recompose. Pattern 1 and 3 would require measuring the entire 50,000-character string every update. Pattern 4 (Column) keeps all paragraphs composed even when off-screen.
hints: Think about what happens to off-screen content in Column vs LazyColumn
50,000 characters means measuring all of it on every recomposition with patterns 1 and 3

questions.title: SelectionContainer + LazyColumn
questions.description: A user tries to select text across two messages in this chat UI. What happens?
options: Selection works perfectly across all messages, like a web page
Selection works only within a single LazyColumn item — you cannot select across multiple items because LazyColumn disposes off-screen items
The code crashes at runtime because SelectionContainer cannot wrap LazyColumn
Selection works across visible items but breaks when scrolling
questions.explanation: SelectionContainer enables text selection, but LazyColumn independently composes and disposes items. Each item is a separate composition scope. Selection cannot span across independently-composed items because their text state may not coexist. You can select within a single message, but not across multiple messages. This is a fundamental limitation of lazy layouts, not a bug.
hints: LazyColumn composes items independently and disposes off-screen items
Think about what happens to item state when items are disposed

questions.title: Recomposition Scope Isolation
questions.description: This chat screen janks badly during streaming because Header and InputField recompose on every token. Fix it so only the response text recomposes.

checks.message: The state collection should happen inside the streaming composable, not in ChatScreen
Pass the Flow/StateFlow down instead of the collected value

questions.explanation: The fix is to move collectAsState() out of ChatScreen and into the StreamingResponse composable. When state is read in ChatScreen, the entire ChatScreen recomposes on every token, causing Header and InputField to recompose too. By passing the StateFlow (not the value) to a child composable and reading it there, recomposition is confined to StreamingResponse only.
hints: Where is collectAsState() called? That composable and all its children will recompose
Pass the StateFlow down, not the collected value

questions.title: derivedStateOf for Streaming
options: It crashes because derivedStateOf cannot reference collectAsState values
It shows only every 100th character of the response
It throttles recomposition: the BasicText only recomposes when the text grows by ~100 characters, not on every single token
It has no effect — displayedText still equals fullText and triggers every recomposition
questions.explanation: derivedStateOf { fullText.length / 100 } uses integer division, so the derived value only changes when length crosses a 100-character boundary (0, 100, 200...). remember(textSnapshot) { fullText } only re-executes when textSnapshot changes, so downstream composables only recompose every ~100 characters instead of every token. The text still shows the full content when it does update — it just updates less frequently.
hints: What does integer division do to the value? When does 4999/100 differ from 5000/100?
remember(key) { value } only recomputes when key changes

questions.title: Blinking Cursor Animation
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a blinking cursor animation for streaming text.
questions.code:
val infiniteTransition = ___1___(label = "cursor")
val cursorAlpha by infiniteTransition.___2___(
    initialValue = 1f,
    targetValue = 0f,
    animationSpec = infiniteRepeatable(
        animation = tween(durationMillis = 500),
        repeatMode = RepeatMode.Reverse
    ),
    label = "blink"
)

blanks.hint: Creates an InfiniteTransition that is remembered across recompositions
Animates a Float value continuously between initial and target

questions.explanation: rememberInfiniteTransition creates a transition that runs indefinitely and is lifecycle-aware (pauses when off-screen). animateFloat continuously animates a float value between initialValue and targetValue. With RepeatMode.Reverse, the alpha oscillates 1→0→1→0... creating the blink effect. The 500ms duration gives a natural cursor blink rate.
hints: The transition needs to be remembered across recompositions and run forever
You're animating a Float value (alpha) for the cursor
